I used to put this down to malicious activity by a hostile neighbor. She lives in the flat below, and has been known to violently awaken me by banging on the floor (her ceiling) just below my bed. But her attacks generally have a discernible pattern, there is a buildup to them. This does not follow that pattern. Also a family member long slept in this room, experiencing the same phenomenon; and she has never been under similar attack from our neighbor. I’ve sometimes wondered if there isn’t a body buried under it or something like that. replies.title: Re: i ching; tarot; astrology; or other?
replies.content: If you're drawn to I Ching, then go for it. Personal preference is as good a guide as any other. One thing about the Book of Changes, though, is that you need to treat it with respect; every oracle has a distinct personality, and the I Ching is notorious for being basically a wise old man who will teach you if you're polite. As for getting training, the rules are the same for any oracle: cast a daily divination asking for guidance for that day, write it down, do your best to interpret it, then go back to it a day later and see how you did. Rinse and repeat, and after a while you'll teach yourself how to understand the oracle.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Quite the contrary. Gerard Thibault's system of Renaissance Pythagorean rapier fencing is extraordinarily efficient in terms of movement -- since it's based on geometry, the principle of least motion governs its movements. I've had the pleasure of watching matches where somebody using Thibault's methods faced somebody using either the contemporary Italian methods or the later French methods; the other guy's capering about, lunging and leaping and crouching, while the guy using Thibault's methods has an easy upright posture, takes measured steps, and has his sword always extended at the other guy. A few clashes of the swords, and then thump -- the other guy takes the point of a rapier straight the middle of his mask; end of match.

2) Not that I know of, but it would be easy to do. My first novel, The Fires of Shalsha, includes a system of mystical handgun fighting as one of the plot elements; it was partly inspired by Thibault and partly by kyudo, the Japanese tradition of archery.

replies.title: choosing a magical system
replies.content:
FWIW, when I first started studying magic I was drawn to telluric and lunar magic and repulsed by or uninterested in solar and stellar magic. My study and practice were self-directed, and I did just what I liked. I made rapid progress for about five years, then got stuck making no progress with certain parts of my personality that needed work, began to feel bored and that I was repeating myself, and losing belief in what I was doing. I concluded that doing nothing but lunar magic long term had given me the psychic equivalent of a vitamin deficiency, so I took a Minerval initiation in the OTO, which in the Man of Earth degrees is intensely solar. This didn't come naturally to me but I stuck with the OTO for a few years and learned some valuable things.

I don't have any advice on what you should start with, but it's worth noticing what parts of your personality are getting developed and which aren't, because in time a lack of balance will undermine your overall power and good sense.

comments.content: I know you haven't systematically studied shamanism, but from what you do know, what would you say are the most significant differences between shamanism and Western occultism? From what I've read they don't seem overwhelming as a lot of people were magicians before they became shamans. And those who weren't, after a few years the spirits seem to start prodding them to learn magic so they can be more independent and do more things on their own.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I haven’t studied shamanism extensively, but I have studied it a bit. I should start by saying traditional shamanic practices vary widely, and so do western mystical practices, so to compare them I have to simplify and generalize things.

In general, shamanism (especially indigenous, native forms of shamanism) is strongly based in trance and the use of entheogens (usually native hallucinogenic plants). Modern shamanism has gotten away from that somewhat, but it has also gotten away from some of the “battling the dark” and “facing the shadow” type elements as well. Drugs are not necessary for shamanic practice, but achieving a deep trance definitely is. It also places a stronger emphasis on interacting with the spirit world directly, and interacting with the physical world by interacting with its equivalent forms in the spirit world (for example, retrieving pieces of a person’s soul in the spirit realm to cure disease).

Western occult practices vary widely in their treatment of trance and the spirit world. Most require a light trance to perform rituals (with visualizations to interact with the spirit and physical worlds at the same time), but few require deeper states of trance (though many allow it, and you can often find things like astral travel or remote viewing offered as a separate course of study like many other topics). A few offer specific courses of study that involve specific tasks in the spirit world, but generally there is a sense that the fully immersive spirit workings are not as deeply integrated with the other practices.

There are exceptions, and Western occultism did start out as the shamanic practices of western Europe. I read a fascinating theory that many of the dark and demonic elements of early western occultism originated because the entheogens available in western Europe were very toxic and unpleasant, so they had a tendency to produce a disproportionate amount of bad trips. Though the author did not take the theory any further, it would also explain why western occultism ditched the entheogens sooner than other cultures and then developed a wider repertoire of techniques that was based on lighter trances.

I hope this helps :)

Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: Re: Drugs and Spirituality in Teens
replies.content: I also took up meditation in my teen years, and found drugs boring by comparison. My recommendation? Teach your daughter to meditate!

replies.title: Re: Drugs and Spirituality in Teens
replies.content:
Not sure how "young adult" 36 is, these days. :P But for me, pot has always been recreational fun, whereas I *mostly* do spiritual stuff sober. (The exception being that a shot or so of rum has enhanced trancework and/or communion once in a while, though in a way that's generally too intense for me to do regularly.)

As a general rule, I've found that use v. abuse works the same with "soft" drugs like pot as it does with alcohol, eating, gambling, relationships, etc.: except for genetic predispositions toward *some* kind of addiction, people are much less likely to pick up serious habits when they have other stuff going on in their lives that they find important and interesting. (This last bit means that parents can't keep their kids off drugs by overscheduling--my memories suggest that the kids who have a different mandatory lesson or game every day after school cross over to the other side, where they'll likely get high either to keep up with everything or to calm their nerves. And rightly so.) The best thing a parent can do, IM-non-parent-O, is encourage genuine non-drug interests.


(And also be aware of potential mental illnesses, which are another contributing factor--lots of people in my extended family tried to treat depression with booze, and it did not end well.)

replies.title: Re: Drugs and Spirituality in Teens
replies.content:
I was exposed to a lot of drugs as a teen and young adult, and I found spirituality just before that. I went through an experimental phase but I also found spirituality more fulfilling and now use the fewest substances of any of my friends or family (though I did use a wider variety of strange plants when I did experiment).

Spirituality can definitely help, but you can’t force your child to take up your spiritual practice (I converted to Paganism from Christianity). You can teach your child and lead by example, but ultimately the child will choose a spiritual (or non spiritual) path of their own.

It’s better to teach how to be responsible, for example: its OK drink a little, getting drunk is bad, never drink and drive, the danger of pot is that it’s illegal and it can ruin your chances for a good career, talk about addiction, talk about impaired judgment and how the consequences of one bad decision can affect the entire rest of your life, talk about how a prescription issued by a doctor can turn into an addiction, use real examples of real people you know whenever you can, and make it perfectly clear that if your kid DOES make a mistake the first thing they should do is call you so you can *help* them. My sister had a policy with her kids, if they made any sort of mistake (like teen drinking) and needed a ride home (to avoid drunk driving) she would go get them, no consequences. Everyone’s parenting style is different, and each kid will respond to a combination of teaching and discipline. I will say, though you have to decide what your policies are and then talk about these things, because the school is telling them a bunch of obvious lies (pot is a dangerous, highly addictive schedule 1 narcotic... they won’t believe anything school says after that sentence) and their friends generally have no idea that bad consequences can happen to them personally.

I hope this helps. And I hope your kid is starting out with good sense (and good friends), so you don’t have to worry about this stuff.

Sincerely,
Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: Re: Drugs and Spirituality in Teens
replies.content:
The knowledge that I found most helpful in avoiding the use of recreational drugs is "there is no quality control". You never really know what's in that herb, pill, or potion, and it might be very different from what it's claimed to be.

That, and the sense that I might some day obtain some unique privilege for never having done a drug, kept me clean when all around me were "partaking". I've never regretted it.

comments.title: Divination
comments.content:
Dear JMG, I would be very interested in your opinion about two related notions I have come across repeatedly: one, that people paying close attention to astrology are going to be more strongly affected by current astrological influences than others ("because they are much more dialed in to the field"); and two, that repeatedly doing divinations to predict a particular outcome is going to lock that future outcome more rigidly in place each time.

The common theme here is, of course, that divination may not just predict effects, but actually serve to strengthen them for better or worse. This would certainly be important to take into account in one's practice, provided that things really work that way. From your experience, would you say they do? Many thanks for your help.

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content:
There are two different issues involved here. With astrology, yes, you start by becoming more susceptible to astrological influences, because you're aware of them; over time, though, you learn how to balance them out with shifts in consciousness and behavior -- for example, if you've got some experience working with astrology, Mercury retrogrades are no trouble at all, because you know what's hindered and what's favored by Mercury when he's retrograde and you adjust your plans and your mental states to go with the astrological flow. Of course you also benefit more from positive astrological conditions -- when Mercury's applying to a trine with Jupiter, say, you know that it's the right time to submit that manuscript or apply for that new job, so you get the benefits that others miss.

As for repeatedly doing divinations about the same question, this is a bad idea, for the same reason that it would be a bad idea to ask me the same question over and over again on this forum. Oracles can get annoyed with you. If you do a divination and the result isn't what you want -- which is usually when this kind of thing happens -- accept that, and don't ask the same question again until and unless something obviously changes in the situation. If you do a second reading, and get the same result, accept it and don't ask again. If you keep on trying to force the oracle to tell you what you want to hear, you risk permanently impairing your capacity to use that oracle, or any other.

replies.title: Re: negative thinking
replies.content:
Every state of consciousness is self-reinforcing. You have a negative and pessimistic outlook, so you tend to notice, think about, and remember all the bad things that have happened to you and your family members; so you behave, consciously or otherwise, in ways that bring more bad things your way, and that reinforces your negative and pessimistic outlook, and around and around you go.

When you say that you're comfortable within a Judeo-Christian framework, does that extend to prayer? If so, that can be a very effective approach for those who aren't drawn to magic. Make a habit of daily prayer, and ask for help getting out of the vicious circle you're in. You can combine prayer with Bible reading and discursive meditation on scriptural passages, making a very effective method of spiritual development.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I've seen claims that cancer is an etheric disease, usually in old occult writings. Do you think there's any truth there?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
All illnesses have at least some etheric dimension, because the etheric level is the level of the life force. Even something that's primarily a result of a physical trauma will have an etheric dimension, since the etheric body responds to injury, toxicity, etc. in parallel with the physical body. In the case of infectious diseases, it's usually a weakness of the etheric body that lets the illness take hold -- that's why not everybody gets whatever cold is going around, and so on. In the case of diseases that are basically malfunctions of the body, the etheric element is even more important.

Cancers vary, though. A lot of cancer is caused by chemical pollutants -- that's why childhood cancer, which was so rare in the 19th century that individual cases got written up in the medical journals, is so much more common now -- and some seems to be caused by electromagnetic pollution as well. So exactly how important the etheric body is in any given cancer seems to vary from one type of cancer to another, and possibly from case to case.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One thing I've noticed is that an awful lot of pollutants linked to cancer are VOCs (volatile organic compounds), which also seems to be common in incense. I wonder if pollution might cause cancers by some sort of interaction on the etheric level.

Also, given that quite a few texts use "magnetism" to mean what I'd call "etheric", there does seem to be a connection there too. I guess further research is needed to figure this out then.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Both of those are entirely plausible. The use of the term "magnetism" as a word for etheric energy was inspired by the fact that magnets have strong etheric as well as physical effects; as Karl von Reichenbach showed through extensive controlled experiments, a good clairvoyant can see the difference between north and south poles in a dark room.

replies.title: Re: Do I really need them?
replies.content:
Those words really do cause problems, don't they? The word "worship" originally was "worthship" -- it simply means recognizing something as worthy. (That's why in countries that got their political system from Britain, the title "Your worship" is standard for mayors and justices of the peace.) I'm not sure where it got the connotation of groveling subservience, but that's certainly not what it means in most magical traditions.

Are you okay with the idea that some of the beings and entities out there are considerably mightier and wiser than human beings, and will help you out if approached with appropriate courtesies? That's the standpoint from which mages approach gods.

replies.title: Re: Astrological transits for dating
replies.content:
To answer that question, I'd have to spend a couple of hours delineating your natal chart, and then work out how the transits relate to your natal chart and your annual progressed chart. This is why astrologers like Kelli Fox charge money for their expertise, and it's also why their schedules tend to be full, because there are only so many hours in a day.

I don't practice astrology professionally, though I've got the skills in place as a fallback in case the writing gig stops paying my bills. If I did, I'd probably charge around $100 for a natal chart delineation and an extra $50 or so to work out the effects of your transits.

I will offer one piece of advice, though. Of the planets you've listed, only one has much to do with dating. That being the case, you only have two transits to worry about, and that should make it much easier to figure out what to do...

replies.title: Re: Astrological transits for dating
replies.content:
My point was simply that I'm not going to put in three hours or so of fairly hard work just to answer a question on Magic Monday.

As for your question point, got it in one. The planets aren't uniform or universal in their effects, and if you're focusing on a specific event such as a speed dating event, you look at the planet or planets that rule the subject of the event: in this case, Venus. Look at your natal chart, and see if there are any planetary aspects in the natal chart to your natal Venus; if they're positive aspects, those are things to play up, while if they're negative aspects, those are things to avoid. (If you've got a Saturn semisquare your natal Venus, for example, be on guard against talking too much about dull subjects and boring your partner.) Then look at the planet that rules the cusp of the house of your natal chart in which Venus is located, and do the same thing with any aspects to that planet. With that in mind, you should be fairly well prepared.

Way back in 1990 I bought a house (a small weaver's home) from 1648 in the historic city of Leiden in the Netherlands. I went round the house in the summer and liked the place, but when I moved in in October of that year, I noticed that it had a certain uncomfortable vibe and I didn't much like being on my own in it. That was mainly solved when my girlfriend moved in with me. She noticed the bad vibe too, but dismissed it just like I did. However, during the seven years we lived there, no end of less-than-pleasant things took place: a family member died unexpectedly in his early 30s precipitating a whole chain of further problems; the husband of the couple I bought the house from was killed in a car accident in Paris, less than a year after I moved in; a young couple moved in to the house next door and within a year the pretty wife made several attempts to take her own life each time she would put a favourite sad song on automatic replay which we could hear through the walls - they had to sell up and move away; in the house on the other side of theirs where another young couple was living, there was an almighty row one day and the husband was summarily kicked out never to return; into the house on the other side of mine a Canadian moved in who seemed to suffer bouts of depression when he would play the same Enya album over and over again whilst hitting the bottle; next to the Canadian a press photographer lived with his wife, she often screamed wildly in anger and one time in a fit of rage scratched all the cars in the street with her keys; our cat was attacked and killed by a pitbull terrier even though these dogs are supposed to be muzzled and on a leash at all times; whilst living there, my girlfriend (later my wife) and I repeatedly had the same dream: that we would go down the passage between the bedrooms and find a hidden door leading to many more rooms; in 1996 when my wife was starting to go into labour with our first child, there was a power failure in just our street, this was sufficiently disturbing for her that her contractions ceased and our daughter had to be artificially induced two days later; starting when our daughter was about six months old, she wouldn't be put down in her cot to sleep in her little room, she would scream and scream and scream as if terrified to be left alone; in 1997 I developed a duodenal ulcer and, putting all my faith in the medical profession, agreed to a course of antibiotics to cure it, since when I have had IBS. There were many more things, but you get the idea.

In 1998 we moved to a modern house and an enormous weight immediately dropped from our shoulders, we had become so used to it we didn't even notice it until it had gone, our daughter, too, was so much happier in the new house. However, the story doesn't quite end there. Something like a year later I was up in the attic and, following an article I had read about intuition, I decided to see if I had any ability. I picked up a pack of ordinary playing cards and tried to guess what colour each was before I turned it over. I would make an initial guess and then "something" would sometimes nudge me to change my mind, other times not. In any case, I got them all right - not just a string of five or ten cards, nope, the whole pack of 52 cards! I was more than a little freaked by this and left the attic shaking slightly. I have never tried to repeat the exercise. The thing is that about ten years ago I started to get interested in the occult and read your books Paths of Wisdom and later Learning Ritual Magic. While doing the LRM lessons, I would often sense a presence in my attic room but didn't know whether it was benign or not. There have been a few minor unpleasant things happen during my attempts at magic (my kidneys shut down for nearly a day once - very scary), especially if I didn't banish or didn't banish properly (to start with doing the LBRP I walked round the wrong way for some strange reason!). So I now wonder if it's possible whether some entity from the old house attached itself to me and is causing the IBS and draining me of energy. If so, what might I do about it? (BTW I stopped doing Golden Dawn style magic and switched instead to druidry as I felt much happier with the Celtic rather than the Judaeo-Christian symbolism and I've always had a thing with trees too.)

There’s quite a lot more I could say about this, but I fear I’ve taken up too much bandwidth already!

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
Incidentally, the current thinking which has come from a wider sampling of microscopic organisms than the big charismatic ones that got a lot of attention in the older days, especially genetic analysis. is the following:

There are three big classes of living things on Earth - the bacteria, the archaea which look just like them but are very chemically different due to an ancient evolutionary branch, and the eukaryotes which came from a union of the two coming back together (!). The bacteria and archaea are fantastically chemically diverse but limited in the shapes/forms they take, while eukaryotes all have pretty much the same chemistry but make up for it by taking different complicated forms and shapes.

Within the eukaryotes there are at least 20+ groups that are as different from each other as the old-school 'kingdoms' of animals, plants, and fungi are from each other. Three of these groups are, of course, the animals, plants, and fungi, each of which learned to become big and multicellular and thus both have a lot of biomass and get a lot of attention from beings like us. There are a few other groups that became multicellular, like kelp, but they did not have the explosive world-changing success of those three big contenders. But there is a fantastic degree of diversity in the little guys, and some of them are rather more genetically complex than we are.

comments.title: Spirits of the Arbatel

What's your opinion on working with the spirits of the Arbatel of Magic?

The book gives a very barebones method of prayer to work with them. How effective is that, compared to using a more complex evocation framework like with the Goetia?

replies.title: Re: Spirits of the Arbatel
replies.content:
The Arbatel is a standard work of Christian magic, and an effective one, but it deliberately leaves out some of the details -- that was standard at the time, to keep the clueless from getting into trouble. If you follow the instructions exactly, including the religious dimension, and fill in the blanks from other commonly available sources of the time, it's a very effective system; you may not get the sort of flashy results a lot of people expect from goetic magic, but the results tend to be more stable and less often boobytrapped.

The prayer in Aphorism 14 should be said daily, preferably when you first wake in the morning; the summonings of specific Olympic spirits should be done in the day and hour of the corresponding planet, when that planet is direct of motion, not in its detriment or fall, and not afflicted by aspect to one of the malefic planets. If you're at all familiar with Renaissance astrological magic, and use those methods to fill in the Arbatel's blanks, that's one very effective way to work the system.

comments.title: Consecration
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Thanks as always for hosting these open forums.

Could you explain what you mean by consecration of objects from your perspective of Western occultism? I think it is one of those terms that gets used to mean different things by various groups. I have seen references to consecrating religious statues and various magical instruments here, but I'm not sure I fully understand the term.

For background, I am particularly interested in the consecration of buildings, especially mundane buildings like houses, which are not primarily religious in function.

Warmly,

samurai_47

replies.title: Re: Consecration
replies.content:
In magical terms, you consecrate something when you mean to set it apart for magical use, and place some specific energy or set of energies into it. For example, to consecrate a magical wand is to charge it with subtle energies so that it will assist you in shaping the forces of the magical cosmos; to consecrate a talisman is to charge it with a specific force so that it will radiate that force to accomplish a particular purpose.

In the magical sense of the term, you don't consecrate anything that's going to have a nonmagical use, and you don't consecrate anything that's going to be handled by anyone but you. If you want to bring positive magical energies into something in common use -- for example, a house -- that's a blessing, not a consecration.

replies.title: Re: Consecration
replies.content:
samurai_47,

Forgive the intrusion...I am thinking you are looking for a foundation-rite, not a consecration formula. If I am right, this may be helpful:

In one of the display rooms dedicated to Ancient Egypt in the British Museum there is a glass case containing small faience models of grape clusters, bread loaves, fish, haunches of meat and other foodstuffs. The card explained that these were "foundation deposits", permanent symbolic offerings buried at each corner of a building's footprint. Maybe this was an abstraction of older folk-magic involving food/drink offered to the local spirits before raising a structure. Beyond that, work on some important buildings began with a foundation-alignment procedure called the Stretching of the Cord. There are murals representing this: they show the Pharaoh and the goddess Seshet (Lady of Records) marking the foundation-line using stakes and rope as the Pharaoh recites a formula about casting his eye to the star.

I'm sure many other groups have traditional rituals of this sort, though you may have to do some research for your planned project.

comments.title: occult musings on crop circles
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. I’m not sure this can be considered a magical or occult question, but I figured it is worth a shot. I have noted from your responses over the years that you clearly believe that crop circles are of human origin (and rightly so). As a lover of geometry I marvel at the sheer beauty, complexity and precision of many of the crop figures and am grateful to the anonymous designers and implementers of this gigantic though ephemeral form of art. But I also wonder how the “circle makers” stay motivated to devote so much time and energy into producing these figures under far-from-ideal circumstances. I doubt that they are raking in the cash by making the figures!

Years ago I was struck by a statement by Patrick Harpur in his book Daemonic Reality that there seems to be a trickster element to the crop circle phenomenon in which the human “circle makers” are under daimonic influence (i.e., they have no clear idea why they are making the crop circles but feel compelled to do so) – and that idea has always stayed with me. Note that I am under no delusion that there is some “divine hand” orchestrating groups of humans to create geometric figures in farmers’ fields in order to prepare humankind for some great spiritual leap, etc., etc., ad nauseum. Still, I can’t shake Harpur’s suggestion and wonder if you have considered the phenomenon from an occult / daimonic perspective.

By the way, thanks for including pics of different occultists on Mondays!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: occult musings on crop circles
replies.content: Oh, it's quite possible, and indeed the people I met in England who were involved in crop circle manufacture were mostly involved in Druidry as well, so more attuned than most to the spiritual side of things. Thing is, for a while there, there was quite a bit of money to be had; busloads of tourists from the US New Age scene would show up and pay a couple of pounds each, day after day, through the summer tourist season -- bringing in a total considerably higher than a farmer would get for a field full of wheat or rapeseed or what have you. I never heard of a farmer having to hire crop circle makers, but the crew could usually count on a great farmhouse breakfast when they finished in the predawn hours, and there were other incentives, most of them liquid in nature. So everyone benefited: the tourists got to ooh and aah at crop circles, the farmers had an extra income stream, and the makers got the thrill of doing the thing, the amusement value of watching self-anointed "cerealogists" announcing that the results of a night's work were unquestionably the product of alien intelligences, and some really good food and drink into the bargain. That there may also have been subtle forces manifesting themselves through all this is by no means contradicted, of course, by these practical issues.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Advertising works by shutting down people's capacity to think and reflect by flooding the mind with crude imagery hammering on a narrow range of primate instincts -- sex, hunger, competition, and the like. replies.title: Re: CGD Geomancy
replies.content:
The version of geomancy in The Celtic Golden Dawn is basically the first half of the version in my geomancy book, so it may be that you tried to go too far too fast. The simple shield chart, as covered in TCGD, is a really effective divinatory method all by itself, and if it works well for you, there's no reason to go to the house chart.

If you're going to do the rest of the Druidical GD system, please do learn the Welsh names. It'll make things much easier for you as further books on the system come out!

The Heathen Golden Dawn project is proceeding step by step. This isn't fast work; it took me five years to create the system in The Celtic Golden Dawn and I knew Druidry and Golden Dawn magic forward and backward; for this project, I know the Golden Dawn stuff and my coauthor knows the Heathen stuff, and it's taking some time to fit them together.

Magical practice does have that effect! Remember that you're working with the life force, and in an age when a lot of people are largely cut off from more than minimal participation in that force, you're like a bubbling spring of water in a desert.

A few weeks ago I asked about the possibility of drugs damaging the aura of an acquaintance of mine. You asked for further details, but I was unable to reply before the cutoff time.

The drug was cocaine. As for my experiences with the person, I'm not sure how to separate out the strands of my previous traumas and bog-standard emotional upset from any kind of subtle body effect.

It was an extremely difficult relationship in general. There were times when I felt that a different (and awful) personality would emerge in the person. There were times when I would feel a buildup of tension and dread in my solar plexus, and I learned to rely on the feeling as an indicator of trouble in the near future.

At one point, I received a split-second image of the person, as though they were possessed in a horror movie - chalk-white skin, screaming, surrounded by darkness and blue flame. This was while I was talking with them, in an otherwise low-key practical conversation.
At the time I chalked it up to having read some horror comics recently - and that might be the case. But it stands out to me years later as a strange incident in a very difficult year.

Also, thank you for providing this forum. It's vastly expanded my horizons, and it's helped me push through a lackadaisacal streak in my personality, to develop a steady spiritual practice.

Thanks,
Cliff

replies.title: Re: drugs and aura damage
replies.content: Hard to tell. I've never been around a serious cocaine user, fortunately. I've known a couple of people who used heroin and they had auras that felt as though they were coated with sand. It was really strange, and very distinctive. Thus it's at least possible that your acquaintance had messed up his aura, and also possible that one or more low-level predatory spirits had latched onto him and were using him as a lunch counter.

comments.title: Is Cannabis fooling me?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

In 2015, as a 30 year old man, I used marijuana for the very first time in my life. Since then, while experimenting with this plant, I have perceived the energies of herbs in my garden and trees in the forest, felt energy moving in and around my body, and even had a meditative encounter with (I presume) the Virgin Mary. Many sources tell me, however, that if I want to go further with these kinds of experiences, which I do, that I will need to learn to do so without the aid of marijuana. I am hoping that magic can be a way for me to accomplish this. But in the meantime, are these sorts of experiences, which come to me while very high, trustworthy at all? Are they accurate perceptions of a reality that hasn’t been available to me in my normal day to day state of consciousness? Or are they an untrustworthy illusion that I shouldn’t put much weight into? Or maybe somewhere in between? Thank you kindly for the opportunity to ask you these questions.

replies.title: Re: Is Cannabis fooling me?
replies.content: Good question. I'm far from expert in drug-based experiences; I dabbled a little in my late teens and early twenties, but found drugs boring compared to meditation and magic; I've read different people making exactly opposite claims about the reality and importance of drug-based experiences, and since I lack any real experience in the field, I'm not really qualified to offer an opinion.

replies.title: Re: Is Cannabis fooling me?
replies.content: It is a fluid interplay of both - one moment they give an accurate perception of a reality, next moment they are untrustworthy illusion that I shouldn’t put much weight into, and most of the time they are both simultaneously.

comments.title: Choosing a practice
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I'm readying myself to start a magical daily practice. I've been dipping in and out of the occult, paganism and Druidry for many years in a mainly theoretico-philosophical way. I have no personal relations with any gods but feel particular affinity with the Tuatha De (particularly Manannan Mac Lir)the Norse gods and the Queen of Elphame from Scottish balladry, as well as a soft spot for the Virgin Mary. I'm trying to choose a ritual to start with and feel inclined to use your polytheist LBRP adapted to the TDD. My question is, How do I know if the deities picked are the right ones? (I've followed the 4 letter rule for the quarters) or, put it this way, is it sensible to change the deities after a while to see if other set works best? Is it also OK to experiment from the start with two pantheons? (TDD and Aesir as per your Heathen LBRP).
Also, regarding divination, I would like to use ogam. In this respect, Are you familiar with the Green Man Tree Oracle by John Matthews? Would it be viable to apply these cards to the ogam divination you present in the DMH? (the set uses the BLFSN order for the first aicme).

Hope this is not too burdensome and may I say many thanks again for devoting so much of your time every single week to help everybody here!

Manuel

replies.title: Re: Choosing a practice
replies.content:
It's important to begin work with one magical system and one pantheon, and not try to do others until you've gotten the basics down solidly -- say, two or three years of daily practice worth of solidly. Choose one and go with it.

You don't know if the deities you pick are "the right ones" because there are no right ones, and no wrong ones. Pick the ones you pick and, again, stick with it.

Once again, if you're going to use John Matthews' version of the Ogham, as a relative beginner, do that on its own terms and don't try to work in stuff from other versions (such as the one in my book). Each oracle, and each version of each oracle, has its own lessons to teach; don't try to play mix-and-match until you've learned all you can learn from the specific system you start with.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Over at Ecosophia, you wrote:

...Watered-down versions of mindfulness meditation, yoga, and similar practices... make up a large part of the spirituality of the privileged these days. The problem with such practices, when they’ve been pried loose from their original context and the challenging connections to spiritual realities those include, is that they become very effective at convincing you that everything is wonderful, even when it’s critical to realize that everything isn’t wonderful and drastic action has to be taken right away to avert catastrophe.

Oh hi, that's me. Once I had been more aware of those "challenging connections", but over the years I drifted away into that watered-down mindfulness. I made a series of very stupid decisions in 2014 and 2015 under the influence of a loosey-goosey, everything-is-okay mindfulness orientation, not to mention the related influence of everybody-is-okay therapy culture. Catastrophe ensued, like it does.

Since then, I've taken up discursive meditation and it seems to be better at keeping my head out of the clouds. I've also been studying the I Ching for a couple of years; that "wise old man" is good about reminding me to slow my roll. Those seem like a decent start, but it's been unplanned and haphazard.

I suspect a lot of us are going to emerge from watered-down "mindfulness" approaches wondering, "What the hell was I thinking?!" What would you recommend for someone in that position?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Unplanned and haphazard is fine. Have you considered doing discursive meditation on the text of the I Ching? Doing a thorough job of that will give you ample fodder for meditations for years, and also give you a much better grasp of the inner workings of the Book of Changes.

More generally, it depends on the person and their situation. For some people the best advice is to stay the frack away from meditation and spirituality altogether until they get their lives in order. For others, regular discursive meditation, divination, and a basic ritual practice is just what the wizard ordered.

comments.title: Ceremonial Magic and Dream Interpretation
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm a long-time reader of your blogs going back to the Archdruid Report -- thank you for opening up new ways of looking at the world, occult and otherwise.

My questions are about the compatibility of ceremonial magic and discursive meditation with an ongoing, daily practice of dream interpretation along the lines laid out here: https://thewildernessroad.wordpress.com/category/four-step-dreamwork/. It has elements in common with a method of divination, but it isn't focused on outward, future events -- it's more about understanding and integrating the different aspects of oneself. The process of coming up with associations for each dream image has some similarities with discursive meditation, but the symbols are internal rather than external.

Two questions:

1) Would it be inadvisable to start working through the system in Learning Ritual Magic or Celtic Golden Dawn while continuing with this practice?

2) Is there any potential conflict between this practice and discursive meditation on a work of occult philosophy (basically, doing the homework on your other blog)?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Ceremonial Magic and Dream Interpretation
replies.content: Good question. There's no a priori way to figure out exactly how two systems of practice will interact; unless some people have tried it, and seen what the results are, you just don't know. If you're willing to take the chance, go for it.
